behavior_summary: Purely combinational 10-bit binary-to-Gray-code encoder. For any input value bin[9:0], the output is gray[9] = bin[9], and for each i from 0 to 8, gray[i] = bin[i+1] XOR bin[i]. Equivalently, gray = bin ^ (bin >> 1), using a logical right shift that inserts 0 above the MSB. The output must reflect any change on bin without clocking or state.
edge_cases: There is no overflow, underflow, saturation, wrap-around, or internal state; every output bit is a direct combinational function of the current input bits.
The MSB is copied directly: gray[9] must always equal bin[9].
The lower 9 bits must each use the adjacent higher input bit: gray[i] = bin[i+1] ^ bin[i] for i = 0..8.
Equivalent whole-vector interpretation is gray = bin ^ (bin >> 1); a testbench should ensure the shift is right-shift by one bit, not left-shift.
Because the spec says purely combinational, output changes are not delayed by a clock or stored state; only normal combinational propagation delay is implied, not cycle-based latency.
Behavior for X/Z input values is not explicitly specified; only 2-state bit-accurate behavior is guaranteed by the natural-language spec.
mutation_risks: MSB implemented incorrectly, especially forcing gray[9] to 0 or XORing it instead of copying bin[9].
One or more bit positions wired to the wrong adjacent input bits, causing index-shift errors.
Using left shift instead of right shift in the vector formula.
Off-by-one mistakes in the loop bounds, especially for gray[8] or gray[0].
A single output bit tied to a constant or omitted from the XOR logic.
Reversed XOR operands are harmless, but using XNOR instead of XOR is a likely mutation.
Output width mismatches or partial assignment leaving some bits un-driven.
Accidentally making the design sequential or latching instead of purely combinational.
